coredata.dc:title: Photoelectrocatalytic degradation of wastewater and simultaneous hydrogen production on copper nanorod-supported coal-based N-carbon dot composite nanocatalysts

Maimaiti, Halidan

Feng, Lirong
Bao, Jianzhao
Zhao, Xuwei
coredata.dc:description: Cotton pulp black liquor (CPBL) is a highly concentrated organic wastewater with complex composition, and its direct discharge is extremely harmful to the environment. As an important industrial raw material, hydrogen (H2) has a wide range of applications in many fields such as energy and chemicals. In the study, the composite catalyst N-CDs/Cu NRs was prepared in two steps: firstly, coal-based nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared by the ammonia hydrothermal cutting method using Xinjiang Wucaiwan coal as the carbon source; secondly, N-CDs were loaded onto the surface of copper nanorods (Cu NRs; prepared by a liquid-phase reduction) by a hydrothermal method. While characterizing the structure of the material, the performance of photoelectrocatalytic (PEC) degradation of CPBL and H2 production using N-CDs/Cu NRs was investigated: this PEC double reaction takes place in an H-type photoreactor, where the anode degrades CPBL and the cathode produces H2. The chemical oxygen demand removal rate (RTCODCr) of CPBL reached 94.33% and the yield of H2 reached 272.0 mmol in 60 min of reaction. The GC–MS results showed that all aldehydes, phenols, acids, amines, ethers, and most alkanes, ketones and esters in CPBL could be degraded after 60 min.

(A) XRD curve of N-CDs, Cu NRs and N-CDs/Cu NRs with different N-CDs content; (B) TG curve of N-CDs and N-CDs/Cu NRs with different N-CDs content (inset in (B) is corresponding DTG curve); (C) Raman spectra of N-CDs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2); (D) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm of Cu NRs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2).
(A) FTIR of N-CDs, Cu NRs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2); (B-F) XPS spectrum of N-CDs (inset) and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2).

(A) DR UV–vis spectra of N-CDs, Cu NRs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2); (B) The calculation of band gap of N-CDs, Cu NRs (inset) and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2); (C) Mott-Schottky plots of N-CDs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2).

(A) The effect of N-CDs loading on RTCODCr and H2 production form CPBL; (B) The effect of photocatalysis and electrocatalysis on RTCODCr and H2 production form CPBL; (C) The effect of the pH of CPBL on RTCODCr and H2 production; (D) The effect of CPBL concentration on RTCODCr and H2 production; (E) H2 isotope test was performed using D2O instead of H2O; (F) Total ion chromatogram (TIC) of CPBL after PEC degradation in the presence of the N-CDs/Cu NRs at different times.

head.ce:title: Photoelectrocatalytic degradation of wastewater and simultaneous hydrogen production on copper nanorod-supported coal-based N-carbon dot composite nanocatalysts

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cotton pulp black liquor (CPBL) is a highly concentrated organic wastewater with complex composition, and its direct discharge is extremely harmful to the environment. As an important industrial raw material, hydrogen (H2) has a wide range of applications in many fields such as energy and chemicals. In the study, the composite catalyst N-CDs/Cu NRs was prepared in two steps: firstly, coal-based nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared by the ammonia hydrothermal cutting method using Xinjiang Wucaiwan coal as the carbon source; secondly, N-CDs were loaded onto the surface of copper nanorods (Cu NRs; prepared by a liquid-phase reduction) by a hydrothermal method. While characterizing the structure of the material, the performance of photoelectrocatalytic (PEC) degradation of CPBL and H2 production using N-CDs/Cu NRs was investigated: this PEC double reaction takes place in an H-type photoreactor, where the anode degrades CPBL and the cathode produces H2. The chemical oxygen demand removal rate (RTCODCr) of CPBL reached 94.33% and the yield of H2 reached 272.0 mmol in 60 min of reaction. The GC–MS results showed that all aldehydes, phenols, acids, amines, ethers, and most alkanes, ketones and esters in CPBL could be degraded after 60 min.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen (H2) is an important industrial raw material, and has a wide range of applications in petrochemical, food processing, fine organic synthesis, aerospace, medicine and other fields [1]. At the same time, H2 is also a promising clean energy [2]. However, it still mainly comes from fossil fuels such as coal, natural gas and petroleum by-products.And, these raw materials are non-renewable resources and will cause serious pollution in the process of development and utilization [3]. Therefore, it is urgent to develop a low-cost and pollution-free H2 production technology.

Cotton pulp black liquor (CPBL) is a high concentration organic wastewater, which is produced by alkaline cooking, beating and rinsing of cellulose in the process of preparing chemical fiber cotton pulp; the composition of CPBL is extremely complex, which contains a large amount of lignin and its derivatives, cellulose and hemicellulose,dyes and inks; it concentrated almost 90% of the pollutants in the process of pulping [4], with strong odor, deep color, high chemical oxygen demand (COD) and strong alkalinity [5]. Conventional wastewater treatment technology is not only difficult to meet the requirements of complete purification, but also has a high-cost. Meanwhile, CPBL is rich in organic matter, and it is a huge waste of resources if it is treated directly as a pollutant.

Currently, the methods for dealing with organic pollutants mainly include chemical methods (chlorination, ozonation), physicochemical, electrochemical and biological methods [6–9]. Although all of them are effective, there also have many problems [10], such as the generation of harmful by-products, high cost of equipment, low efficiency. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a simple, effectively and environmentally friendly method.

Photocatalytic degradation of pollutants and H2 production technology, as a novel “green” technology to solve the social development problems such as environmental pollution and energy shortage, has attracted widespread attention [11]. As known, the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants is the process of oxidation of organic matter by holes (h+), which are generated on the catalyst surface by photoexcitation. However, it will leave electrons (e-).If not collected in time, the excess e- will compound with h+, reducing the efficiency of photocatalytic degradation of pollutants [12]. The H2 production is the process of reducing H+ using the remaining e- in the system, which is beneficial to reduce the combination of e- and h+ in the process of h+ oxidative degradation of organic matter, thereby improving the efficiency of photocatalytic degradation of organic matter.

Photoelectrocatalytic (PEC) degradation of pollutants and simultaneous production of H2 technology is a new technology that combines electrocatalysis with the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants and simultaneous production of H2 technology. In this process, the reaction efficiency can be greatly improved due to its synergistic effect. A bias potential is applied between the anode and cathode to prevent charge recombination during the photocatalytic reaction, which improves the separation efficiency of the photogenerated e--h+ pairs, and greatly increases the efficiency of the photocatalyst in degrading organic pollutants [13]. At the same time, the photocatalyst is fixed on the anode conductive substrate, which reduces the difficulty of catalyst recovery [14] (In the process of photocatalytic degradation of pollutants, the catalyst is often suspended in the pollutants. This is conducive to the full mixing of the catalyst in the reaction system, but the separation and recovery of the catalyst are difficult, and it is easy to cause photocatalyst poisoning [15]).

Copper nanorods (Cu NRs) have a local surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effect. The unique rod shape and nano size make it have a large specific surface area. Therefore, Cu NRs have stronger redox ability and photocatalytic activity than ordinary Cu metals, and have important application value in the optical field [16]. Carbon dots (CDs) doped with hetero elements can effectively separate photogenerated carriers and play an important role in improving the photocatalytic activity of materials [17]. Nitrogen is the most common and easily doped heteroatom in CDs. The reason may be that its atomic size is comparable to that of carbon [18]. Studies have shown that nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) can improve the photoelectric properties and chemical reactivity of CDs [19], thereby effectively extending the photoinduced carrier lifetime of the materials. Therefore, we believe that loading N-CDs on the surface of Cu NRs is expected to obtain new high-performance PEC materials.

In view of the above considerations, in this paper, highly dispersed coal-based N-CDs were prepared by the ammonia hydrothermal cutting method using Wucaiwan coal as carbon source. The N-CDs/Cu NRs composite catalysts were prepared by loading N-CDs on the surface of Cu NRs (prepared by liquid-phase reduction of CuSO4) by hydrothermal method. The structure and properties of the material were characterized, and the process and mechanism of PEC degradation of CPBL and H2 production were studied.

In this paper, CPBL is used as a research model. Degradation of CPBL and production of H2 can not only reduce the pollution of CPBL to the environment, but also use the organic matter in CPBL as a raw material or sacrificial agent for the preparation of H2. This realizes the concept of turning CPBL into treasure, which has very important research value and practical significance.

Coal samples, provided by Wucaiwan Coal Mine in the Zhundong Coalfield, Xinjiang, China, were ground, passed through 200 mesh, and dried at 105 °C for 8 h. Copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O), ascorbic acid (C6H8O6), hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (C19H42BrN), urotropine (C6H12N4), ammonium hydroxide (NH3·H2O) were obtained from Tianjin Zhiyuan Reagent Co., Ltd. Spectrumiabs dialysis bag (MW 100), provided by Shanghai Tongshan Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Nitrogen (N2, 99.999%) and hydrogen (H2, 99.999%) were purchased from Urumqi Keyuan Gas Manufacturing Co., Ltd. The CPBL, homemade (cotton linter (provided by China’s Xinjiang Fulda Fiber Co., Ltd.) was placed in 5% NaOH solution (solid–liquid ratio: 1:6, mass ratio). After cooking at 180 °C for 12 h, the precipitate was filtered and neutralized with H2SO4 to pH = 7, CODCr = 62922.21 mg L-1, refrigerated for later use).

The ammonia hydrothermal cutting method was employed to prepare coal-based N-CDs as follows: 0.5 g of coal sample was weighed into a conical flask, and then 50 mL NH3·H2O was added. After ultrasonic dispersion for 1 h, the mixture was placed in a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene hydrothermal kettle for the reaction at 180 °C for 12 h. After the solution was cooled to room temperature, the coal cinder was removed by centrifugation, and then the excess ammonia in the filtrate was removed by rotary evaporation. Finally, it was placed in a dialysis bag and dialysis in water for 72 h (constant water changes during the period) until the pH value reaches 7. This process generates an aqueous dispersion of coal-based N-CDs. The product is desiccated to obtain the black powder target product coal-based N-CDs.

The liquid-phase reduction method was employed to prepare Cu NRs as follows [20]: firstly, 0.2 g of CuSO4·5H2O was weighed in a conical flask, 60 mL of distilled water was added to dissolve it, and then 0.4 g C6H8O6, 0.4 g C19H42BrN and 0.4 g C6H12N4 were added under stirring. After stirring for 30 min, it was placed in an oil bath at 80 °C and reacted for 3 h. Then, after washing with water and C2H5OH, centrifugal separation, drying (80 °C, 6 h) and other steps, a purple-red powder is obtained, which is the target product Cu NRs.

The N-CDs/Cu NRs is prepared by a hydrothermal method [21]: 100 mg of Cu NRs and a certain weight of N-CDs were dispersed into 40 mL of water and subjected to ultrasonic dispersion for 1 h. Then, 20 mL of C2H5OH was added into the mixture, and mixed in a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene hydrothermal reactor and reacted at 100 °C for 5 h. When the reaction completed, the product N-CDs/Cu NRs can be obtained through the cooling, filtration, washing, and drying (80 °C, 6 h). Different amounts of N-CDs (5, 10, 15 and 20 mg) provided composite photoelectric-catalysts with different contents of N-CDs/Cu NRs: 5%, 10%, 15% and 20%-N-CDs/Cu NRs are denoted as SP1, SP2, SP3 and SP4, respectively.

The ITO conductive glass (size is 40*20*1.1 mm, sheet resistance is ca. 14 Ω cm−2) was used as a substrate and immersed in a mixed H2SO4/H2O2 solution with a volume ratio of about 3:1 for 3 h [22]. Then, the substrate was cleaned with plenty of deionized water to make the surface more hydrophilic, and place it in a dry place for backup.

First, 12 mg N-CDs/Cu NRs were added into 1 mL of C2H5OH and 50 μL of Nafion solution. Then, after ultrasonic dispersed for 1 h, they were dropped on the treated ITO glass. Finally, the photoelectric anodes were obtained by natural air drying and drying at 80 °C for 8 h.

After the ethanol-dispersed droplets of each sample dried on the copper mesh, the morphological structure of samples was observed using FEI Talos F200s transmission electron microscope (TEM). XRD results of each sample were obtained by using M18XHF22-SRA X-ray diffractometer (experimental conditions: Cu Kα radiation λ = 0.154056 nm, step size: 0.02°, sweep rate: 4°/min, scan range: 2θ = 10-80°). The TG-DTG results of product were got via PE-DTA/1700 thermal analyzer, the temperature range: 25 °C-1000 °C, atmosphere: N2, crucible: Al2O3. Raman spectra were collected by a Raman spectrometer (BHORIBAJYLabRAMHR800) (experimental conditions: laser wavelength: 532 nm, wavenumber range: 50–4000 cm−1). The N2 adsorption–desorption performance was tested by a specific surface and aperture distribution meter (Autosorb-IQ2), and the specific surface and aperture distribution were calculated by the BET and BJH method. The FTIR spectrum of the product was obtained by a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Bruker-EQUINOX 55) (experimental conditions: KBr tablet, wavenumber range: 4000–400 cm−1, scan number: 32, resolution: 4 cm−1). UV–vis absorption spectra were collected by a Shimadzu UV-3100 diffuse reflectance spectrometer, with BaSO4 as a reference, and the test wavelength range was 200–800 nm (experimental conditions: scan speed: 600 nm/min, sampling interval: 0.50 nm). The surface chemical properties were studied using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo K-Alpha + ), with experimental conditions of AlK alpha radiation (1486.6 eV), spot size of 400 μm, analysis chamber pressure of 2*10-9 mbar, and photoelectron pass energy of 30.0 eV. The sample was attached to the conductive adhesive before the XPS test. The electron flood gun was turned on to eliminate the inhomogeneity of charge on the sample surface while the test. CHI 660E electrochemical workstation was used to test the electrochemical impedance (EIS), transient photocurrent-time (i-t) curve, Mott-Schottky curve, linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves, Tafel curves, open circuit potential-time (OCPT) curves and Current density–voltage (J-V) curves of each sample, among them, i-t, Mott-Schottky, EIS, J-V and OCPT were measured in 0.5 mol L-1 Na2SO4 solution. Tafel and LSV were determined in 0.5 mol L-1 H2SO4 solution. A high-resolution gas analysis mass spectrometer (NU Evolution) was used to detect the isotopes of gas samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEC degradation of CPBL and simultaneous production of H2 using N-CDs/Cu NRs
ce:section.ce:para: The experiment of PEC degradation of CPBL and simultaneous production of H2 using N-CDs/Cu NRs was carried out in an H-type PEC reaction cell as shown in Fig. 1 (the material of the cathode and anode reaction chamber in the reaction cell is quartz glass). The experimental steps are as follows:

200 mL of 0.05 mol L-1 Na2SO4 and 190 mL of 0.05 mol L-1 Na2SO4 containing 10 mL of CPBL were added to the cathode and anode chambers of the PEC reactor shown in Fig. 1, respectively. The N-CDs/Cu NRs photoelectrode plate and copper plate were installed on the anode and cathode, respectively. Then, in order to remove oxygen in the solution, N2 is passed through the PEC reactor for 30 min before the reaction. Subsequently, the reactor electrodes were connected to an external power supply (LW-3005 KDS) with a high-precision regulated voltage. The reaction of PEC degradation of CPBL and H2 production was carried out by irradiating the photoanode with a Hg lamp (the high-pressure Hg lamp (250 W) was placed 30 cm away from the photoanode plate) and applying a bias voltage of 7 V. During the reaction, liquid and gas samples were taken every 15 min, and the following tests were performed:

(i) The chemical oxygen demand (CODcr, mg/L) of the liquid samples with different PEC reaction times was determined by the potassium dichromate method, and then the COD removal rate (RTCODcr) was calculated.
(ii) The CPBL before and after PEC degradation was detected by GC–MS to analyze the changes of its organic compounds.

(iii) The gas product produced by the photocathode was injected into the gas chromatograph (GC) to confirm the type of gas sample, and the change of gas sample yield with PEC reaction time is studied by the standard curve method.
(iv) In order to confirm the advantages of PEC in degrading CPBL and producing H2, single electrocatalytic and photocatalytic degradation experiments were carried out, respectively.

In addition, this experiment also used deuterated water (D2O) instead of deionized water to prepare CPBL. According to the aforementioned experimental method, the experiment of PEC degradation of CPBL and simultaneous production of H2 was carried out, and the source of H2 in the PEC process was discussed.

The GC and GC–MS analysis methods and conditions of gas and liquid samples, CODCr detection method and RTCODCr calculation formula of liquid samples, and standard curve method for calculating gas phase product are available in Supporting Information.

To discuss the mechanism of PEC degradation of CPBL, p-benzoquinone (BQ, 2 mmol L-1), terephthalic acid (TA, 2 mmol L-1) and disodium edetate (EDTA-2Na, 2 mmol L-1) were used as the trapping agent for active species:
O2 –,
OH and h+, respectively. The active species capture experiments were carried out under the same reaction conditions as described above. In addition, the nitrotetrazolium blue transformation (NBT) and benzoic acid hydroxylation methods were used to detect the presence of
O2 – and
OH in the PEC reaction.

The morphology and structure of the prepared samples were observed by TEM, and the results are shown in Fig. 2 A-C. As shown in Fig. 2A, the coal-based N-CDs are spherical shape, revealing an average size of ∼ 5 nm, with high dispersion. The HRTEM images show that coal-based N-CDs have lattice fringes similar to the structure of graphite (inset of Fig. 2A). The lattice interval is 0.34 nm, which is close to the (002) crystal plane of graphite [23]. Fig. 2B shows that the morphology of Cu NRs is rod-shaped with a diameter of about 100 nm. As shown in Fig. 2C, N-CDs/Cu NRs are formed by coal-based N-CDs uniformly loaded on the surface of Cu NRs. Further, the HRTEM (the inset of Fig. 2C) shows the d-spacing of the outer layer of N-CDs/Cu NRs is 0.34 nm, which is consistent with the (002) lattice plane of coal-based N-CDs, and the two lattice spacings of the inner layer are 0.212 nm and 0.178 nm, which are consistent with the (111) and (200) planes of Cu NRs [16], respectively. The TEM characterization results prove that the prepared N-CDs/Cu NRs are composite nanomaterials formed by coal-based N-CDs loaded on the surface of Cu NRs.

To determine the composition and elemental distribution of the prepared samples, EDS tests were performed on coal-based N-CDs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) (Fig. 2D), and EDS-mapping test was carried out on N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) (Fig. S2). The distribution of C (55.72%), N (9.14%), O (33.01%) and Si (2.13%) on the surface of coal-based N-CDs was confirmed by EDS (the inset of Fig. 2D), indicating that the skeleton structure of coal-based N-CDs is formed by C linked with O. The emergence of N element indicates the successful doping of N element in the preparation of coal-based CDs during the cutting of coal with ammonia. The Si element comes from coal. The N-CDs/Cu NRs are mainly composed of C (28.85%), N (1.78%), O (9.90%), Si (0.21%) and Cu (59.26%). The high content of Cu in N-CDs/Cu NRs indicates that coal-based N-CDs have been successfully loaded onto the surface of Cu NRs. Fig. S2 shows the distribution of the five elements in the composite: C, N, O, Si and Cu. It can be seen that Cu is mainly distributed in rods, while C, N, O and Si are uniformly distributed on the Cu NRs and also at the edges of the Cu NRs, indicating that the coal-based N-CDs are uniformly distributed on the surface of the Cu NRs.

The crystal phase structures of the prepared catalysts were studied by XRD. Fig. 3 A shows the XRD patterns of coal-based N-CDs, Cu NRs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP1-SP4), respectively. It can be seen that the coal-based N-CDs has a broad and strong diffraction peak near 2θ = 26° and a weak peak near 2θ = 41°, which match well with the (002) and (100) crystal planes of graphitic carbon [24], respectively. The XRD of coal-based N-CDs shows a slight red shift of the broad peak corresponding to the graphitized carbon (002) crystal plane compared to the XRD of the general CDs, which may be due to the introduction of nitrogen-containing groups in the interlayer space [25]. Moreover, there are no other impurity peaks, indicating that the main framework of the coal-based N-CDs prepared in this experiment is relatively pure graphitized carbon.

The XRD patterns of Cu NRs is shown in Fig. 3A. There is a dominant peak at 43.3°, which identifies the face-centered cubic (111) reflection of Cu (JCPDS. No. 04-0836) and two weak peaks at 50.4° and 74.1° corresponding to the reflects of (200) and (220) facets, respectively. The diffraction peaks at 2θ = 36.4°, 42.3°, 61.3° correspond to Cu2+ impurities, which could be the oxidation of Cu (0) when Cu NRs are exposed to air [26].

After loading coal-based N-CDs onto the surface of Cu NRs, the diffraction pattern of N-CDs/Cu NRs has a good match with that of the Cu NRs, and there is no obvious peak corresponding to coal-based N-CDs. It may be due to the characteristic peaks of the N-CDs in the composite being covered by the characteristic peaks of Cu NRs. By comparing the XRD patterns of N-CDs/Cu NRs with different N-CDs loadings, it can be seen that diffraction peaks of SP2 and SP3 correspond to pure Cu (111) and Cu (200) crystal planes with 2θ = 43.3° and 50.4°, which are stronger than those of SP1 and SP4, indicating that the proper loading of coal-based N-CDs contributes to the integrity of the Cu NRs crystal structure.

Fig. 3B shows the TG curves of coal-based N-CDs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP1-SP4) with different N-CDs contents. Under 150 °C, coal-based N-CDs showed a weight loss peak of water. At 290–600 °C, corresponding to the decomposition or dehydration condensation process of functional group [27] such as carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino in coal-based N-CDs. At 600–800 °C, the weight loss peak corresponds to the condensation and dehydrogenation of aromatic structures [28] with semi-coke appearing. The results show that the main components of coal-based N-CDs are aromatic structural substances linked to oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing groups. After loading coal-based N-CDs on the surface of Cu NRs, the weight loss peaks of N-CDs/Cu NRs are lower than those of coal-based N-CDs, which may be due to the lower N-CDs content in N-CDs/Cu NRs.

Raman spectroscopy can be an effective means to characterize the defect structure of carbon nanomaterials. In the Raman spectrum of coal-based N-CDs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) shown in Fig. 3C, the two distinct peaks at 1379 cm−1 and 1601 cm−1 are signed as the D-band and G-band, which arise from the sp3 disordered carbon and sp2 ordered carbon [29], respectively. In general, the ratio of the peak intensity of the D-band to the G-band (ID/IG) indicates the possible degree of graphitization, and a larger value indicates a higher degree of defects in the carbon material. In Fig. 3C, the ID/IG was calculated to be 0.96 and 1.01 for N-CDs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2), respectively. This indicates that the disordered domain of N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) can increase when the surface of Cu NRs is loaded with coal-based N-CDs. This means that the defect concentration of N-CDs/Cu NRs is higher than that of pure coal-based N-CDs.

According to the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms, the formation of mesoporous structure for the Cu NRs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) were observed. The specific surface area of the sample was calculated using the BET equation, and the pore size and distribution was calculated by the BJH equivalent cylinder model, as shown in Fig. 3D. Obviously, the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms for coal-based N-CDs and N-CDs/Cu NRs are the type-IV isotherms. It can be seen that the adsorption of N2 by N-CDs/Cu NRs is much higher than that of Cu NRs, and a typical H3-type hysteresis loop appears after P/P0 = 0.48, which indicates that N-CDs/Cu NRs is a mesoporous material [30]. The mesopores may be formed by the accumulation of coal-based N-CDs on the surface of Cu NRs. Due to the formation of mesopores, the BET increased from 20.58 to 36.32 m2 g−1, and the total pore volume decreased from 0.0210 to 0.1091 m2 g−1. The larger specific surface area and pore volume are favorable for the adsorption of reactants, and can significantly improve the PEC activity [31]. Therefore, it is important for heterogeneous photocatalytic reactions.

To further investigate the chemical structure and formation mechanism of the material, FTIR and XPS tests were carried out on the prepared samples, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . The FTIR results of coal-based N-CDs, Cu NRs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) are shown in Fig. 4A. It can be seen that the stretching vibration bands of N-CDs/Cu NRs at 781 cm−1, 1190–1093 cm−1, 1495–1405 cm−1, 1658–1532 cm−1, 2996–2902 cm−1 and 3540–3320 cm−1 demonstrated the presence of CH2, HN, CO (aldehyde), amide bond (CONH), CH and NH/OH [32–35] in the structure of N-CDs, respectively. The broad absorption band representing the OH formed by the adsorption of water molecules on the surface of Cu NRs appears at 3300–3700 cm−1. These results indicate that: (i) Coal-based N-CDs and Cu NRs are successfully combined by hydrothermal method; (ii) The surface of N-CDs/Cu NRs is rich in oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino and epoxy groups brought by coal-based N-CDs. Compared with coal-based N-CDs, N-CDs/Cu NRs have significantly stronger absorption peaks at 3540–3320 cm−1 and 1658–1532 cm−1, corresponding to NH/OH and CONH, respectively; the absorption peaks of CO (aldehyde) and H-N at 1495–1405 cm−1 and 1190–1093 cm−1 were weakened; the absorption peak of CO at 879 cm−1 disappeared. This may be due to the formation of –CONH- (amide bond) after the CO functional group on the surface of N-CDs was reduced to CO during the hydrothermal synthesis process. The above facts indicate that the as-prepared CDs is N-doped and exhibit abundance hydrophilic groups on the surface.

XPS provides greater insight into the composition and chemical state of the catalyst. The XPS survey spectrum attested to the existence of C, N, O, and Cu with different atomic compositions. Fig. 4B shows the XPS spectra of N-CDs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2). It can be seen that N-CDs and N-CDs/Cu NRs have three strong peaks at 285.3 eV (C1s), 399.1 eV (N1s) and 531.6 eV (O1s) [36], respectively. In addition, N-CDs/Cu NRs also showed a characteristic peak of Cu 2p at 933.1 eV, indicating that N-CDs are mainly composed of C, N and O, while N-CDs/Cu NRs are composed of C, N, O and Cu. This result is consistent with the EDS results, which further proves the elemental composition of N-CDs and N-CDs/Cu NRs.

The XPS spectra and deconvoluted fitted peaks for C, N and O are shown in Fig. 4C-E, respectively. The C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 4C) shows four main peaks with binding energy of 284.7 eV (CC/CC), 285.3 eV (CN), 286.3 eV (CO) and 288.4 eV (CONH) [37], respectively. The above deconvoluted fitted spectra indicate that the N-CDs and N-CDs/Cu NRs are rich in N and O elements. Comparing N-CDs and N-CDs/Cu NRs, it can be observed that after hydrothermal synthesis, the CO peak of N-CDs/Cu NRs disappeared, the intensity of CC/CC peak decreased, and the CN and CONHpeaks increased slightly. This is consistent with the FTIR results.

The XPS spectrum of N 1s (Fig. 4D) revealed the presence of pyridinic-N (398.6 eV), amine-N (399.6 eV) and pyrrole-N (400.4 eV) [37], respectively. Apparently, the peak representing pyridine N in N-CDs is enhanced in the complexes by the hydrothermal reaction, while the peak representing pyrrole N is diminished. It may be due to the conversion of pyrrole N to pyridine N in N-CDs. This shows that the N atoms in N-CDs are successfully incorporated into N-CDs/Cu NRs in different modalities.

The O 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 4E) illustrated three main peaks with the binding energies of 531.5 eV (CO), 532.2 eV (COH/CO) and 533.5 eV (OH) [38], respectively. After the hydrothermal reaction, the intensity of the peak representing COH/CO in N-CDs/Cu NRs decreased, while the intensity of the peak representing CO and OH increased. This is consistent with the analysis of the C1s spectra, which further indicating that the CO functional group on the carbon skeleton of N-CDs/Cu NRs has been reduced to CO in the amide bond.

To further confirm the results of synthesis, Fig. 4F shows the high-resolution Cu 2p spectrum of the N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2), and the main peaks are Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 at 932.3 and 952.1 eV [39], respectively. In addition, the observable satellite peaks appearing in the range of 937–949 eV belong to Cu2+ [40]. Overall, the binding energy values of the samples were not significantly different from those of the Cu (0) state, indicating that the Cu NRs were basically not oxidized by the hydrothermal reaction.

Light and electricity are essential energy sources in the PEC degradation of pollutants. Among them, light has a dominant role in exciting the catalytic material to produce photogenerated carriers, and electricity helps to improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers. Therefore, in order to better investigate the PEC activity of the materials, we investigated the optical properties of the prepared products, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 .

Light absorption is one of the essential factors that determine the PEC performance of electrode materials. Therefore, the optical properties of coal-based N-CDs, Cu NRs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) were analyzed by UV–vis DRS diffuse reflectance spectra. As shown in Fig. 5A, Cu NRs have a strong light absorption intensity in the range below 570 nm, while N-CDs have a lower absorption intensity than Cu NRs, but it is absorbed in the UV and NIR regions. Compared to the N-CDs, light absorption intensity and range of N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) have increased. The reason for this may be that the presence of N-CDs reduces its band gap. To confirm this inference, we calculated the band gap values by the Kubelka-Munk function. That is, the relationship between the F(R∞) and the incident light wavelength λ of coal-based N-CDs, Cu NRs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) (where F(R∞) is the total reflection coefficient) is obtained through the Kubelka-Munk function. Then, the relationship curves of [F(R∞) ·hν]2-hν (direct bandgap of N-CDs and N-CDs/Cu NRs) and [F(R∞) ·hν]1/2-hν (indirect bandgap of Cu NRs) were calculated by Eq. (1) [41] and (2) [27], respectively. Finally, the band gap values of coal-based N-CDs, Cu NRs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) are obtained by extending the tangent of the curve to F(R∞) = 0, which are 1.32 eV, 2.16 eV and 2.02 eV for coal-based N-CDs, Cu NRs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2), respectively (Fig. 5B). It can be seen that the band gap value of N-CDs/Cu NRs is decreased compared to the band gap energy of Cu NRs. This indicates that the loading of N-CDs on the surface of Cu NRs leads to enhanced photo-responsiveness of the composite catalyst, which will contribute to the generation of photogenerated carriers.
(2) F(R∞) = B(hν-Eg)1/2/hν (1); F(R∞) = B(hν-Eg) 2/hν
Where hν is the photon energy, Eg is the band gap, and B is a constant.

In the Mott-Schottky curve, 1/CSC 2 is a function of electrochemical potential, and has a linear relationship with E. The catalyst is a p-type semiconductor material if the slope of the Mott-Schottky curve is negative; otherwise, it is an n-type semiconductor material [42]. Fig. 5C are the Mott-Schottky curve of N-CDs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2). It can be seen that the slope of the Mott-Schottky curves of N-CDs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) are positive, indicating that N-CDs and N-CDs/Cu NRs are typical n-type semiconductor materials. The intersection points of the extrapolated lines of N-CDs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) with the X-axis are −0.5 eV and −0.85 eV, indicating that their conduction band potentials (ECB) are −0.5 eV and −0.85 eV, respectively. We calculated from Fig. 5B that the Eg of N-CDs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) are 1.32 eV and 2.02 eV, respectively. It can be calculated that the valence band potentials (EVB) of N-CDs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) are 0.82 eV and 1.17 eV, respectively.

In order to understand the effect of N-CDs on the charge transfer ability of Cu NRs, the electrochemical properties of N-CDs, Cu NRs and N-CDs/Cu NRs were tested and analyzed, and the results are shown in Fig. 6 .

In general, the semi-circular arc of EIS curve represents the impedance of the catalyst [43]. In the EIS test, the smaller radius of the semicircular arc indicates the lower resistance and the stronger electron transfer ability. Therefore, to investigate the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs of the prepared products, we tested the EIS curves of N-CDs, Cu NRs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) at open circuit potential, as shown in Fig. 6A. The impedance of N-CDs/Cu NRs is smaller than that of pure N-CDs and Cu NRs. This indicates that the introduction of N-CDs can enhance the transfer of the interface charges, and effectively prevent the recombination of the photogenerated e- and h+.

Transient photocurrent-time spectrum was used to further illustrate the transfer characteristics of photogenerated carriers of photocatalysts [31]. The photocurrent-time (i-t) test results of N-CDs, Cu NRs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) are shown in Fig. 6B. It can be seen that the i-t curves of N-CDs, Cu NRs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) have good reproducibility. Moreover, the photocurrent density of N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) is much larger than that of single N-CDs and Cu NRs, which is caused by the transfer properties of N-CDs for photogenerated electrons as mentioned before.This indicates the composite catalyst N-CDs/Cu NRs is more easily excited by light.

The electrocatalytic performance of N-CDs, Cu NRs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) was evaluated using a standard three-electrode system, and the results are shown in Fig. 6C. At a current density of 10 mA• cm−2, the overpotential of N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) was 30.8 mV, which was lower than the overpotential of N-CDs (43 mV) and Cu NRs (40.2 mV), indicating that N-CDs/Cu NRs had better HER electrocatalytic performance at the same current density [44].

In principle, a lower Tafel slope signifies a lower applied overpotential required to generate a sufficient current, which acts as determining step towards the kinetics of HER reaction [45]. As can be seen from the Tafel curves shown in Fig. 6D, the slope of the Tafel curve for N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) (21.4 mV/dec) is smaller than that for N-CDs (40 mV/dec) and Cu NRs (28.8 mV/dec). The results indicate that N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) is an efficient catalyst for HER reaction. This result is consistent with the LSV test results (Fig. 6C), which further demonstrates that N-CDs/Cu NRs has superior HER reaction kinetics compared with N-CDs and Cu NRs. Apparently, the improved HER performance of N-CDs/Cu NRs is attributed to the efficient charge transport capability of the N-CDs loaded on the surface of Cu NRs.

The open-circuit potential and time curve (OCPT) (Fig. 6E), which monitors the dynamic nature of the charge carriers [44]. It can be seen from the figure that N-CDs, Cu NRs and N-CDs/Cu NRs all have relatively stable photovoltage. Among them, N-CDs/Cu NRs have the highest photovoltage response and the smallest composite surface state [46].

Fig. 6F shows the current density–voltage (J-V) curves of N-CDs, Cu NRs and N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) under light and dark conditions, respectively. It is clear that more carriers are generated under light conditions than dark conditions; under the same light or dark conditions, the current density of N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) is higher than that of N-CDs and Cu NRs. This further demonstrates that the loading of N-CDs results in a significant improvement in the carrier separation of N-CDs/Cu NRs.

The electrochemical performance tests results shown in Fig. 6 are consistent with the optical performance tests results of the prepared products shown in Fig. 5, indicating that the prepared N-CDs/Cu NRs have better photoelectric properties after loading N-CDs onto the surface of Cu NRs. It can be speculated that the N-CDs/Cu NRs will have higher activity of PEC degradation of CPBL and H2 production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The performance of PEC degradation of CPBL and simultaneous production of H2
ce:section.ce:para: According to the above experimental method, a 250 W Hg lamp was selected as the light source, and CPBL was used as the pollutant. The PEC degradation of CPBL and H2 production experiments were performed using N-CDs/Cu NRs. During the experiment, it was found that the color of black-brown CPBL became fade, and gas was generated at the photoanode and cathode. As shown in Fig. S3, gas products are detected by GC. The GC diagram shows that the gases produced in the anode chamber are O2 and CO2, while only H2 is produced in the cathode chamber. It is not difficult to speculate that O2, CO2 and H2 should come from the degradation of CPBL and the decomposition of H2O in the system. Therefore, we first used the RTCODCr of CPBL and the yield of H2 as indicators to study the performance of PEC to degrade CPBL and produce H2, and the results are shown in Fig. 7 A.

Fig. 7A shows the changes of RTCODCr of CPBL and H2 yields with degradation time. When coal-based N-CDs and Cu NRs were used as catalysts alone, the RTCODCr of CPBL and H2 yields were not high. In contrast, the N-CDs/Cu NRs showed higher PEC degradation and H2 production activity. When SP2 was chosen as the catalyst, the RTCODCr of the CPBL reached 94.33% and the yield of H2 was 272.0 mmol after 60 min of PEC reaction. Compared with N-CDs and Cu NRs, the RTCODCr of SP2 is 2.2 and 1.6 times that of N-CDs and Cu NRs, respectively, and the output of H2 is 1.68 and 1.5 times. It can also be seen from Fig. 7A that N-CDs/Cu NRs with different coal-based N-CDs content have different abilities to degrade CPBL and produce H2. With the change of light time, the RTCODCr and H2 production capacities of the catalysts are consistent: SP2 > SP3 > SP1 > SP4 > Cu NRs > N-CDs. The results showed that N-CDs/Cu NRs had the strongest ability of PEC to degrade CPBL and produce H2 when the loading of N-CDs was 10% of Cu NRs.

To demonstrate the advantages of the PEC system in degrading pollutants and simultaneously producing H2, under the same experimental conditions, the photocatalytic and electrocatalytic experiments were carried out, and the results are shown in Fig. 7B. It can be seen that the RTCODCr and the amount of H2 are not high when using photocatalysis and electrocatalytic to degradate CPBL (in the PEC process, RTCODCr is 3.42 and 2.48 times of the photocatalytic and electrocatalytic processes, respectively, and the amount of H2 is 1.59 and 1.33 times). This shows that PEC combines the advantages of photocatalysis and electrocatalysis, and has greater advantage in degrading CPBL and simultaneously producing H2.

In order to investigate the effect of pH on RTCODCr and H2 production, we adjusted the pH of CPBL from 3 to 11, and performed experiments under the same experimental conditions as above. The results are shown in Fig. 7C. It can be seen that the highest RTCODCr of CPBL and H2 production were achieved when the pH = 9 of CPBL. This indicates that when CPBL is weakly alkaline, N-CDs/Cu NRs has the best performance for PEC degradation of CPBL and H2 production. This may be due to the greater solubility of organic substances such as lignin and cellulose in CPBL under alkaline conditions, which is beneficial to PEC degradation and H2 production.

To investigate the effect of the initial concentration of CPBL on the PEC degradation of CPBL and H2 production, CPBL was diluted to obtain different concentrations of CPBL, and then the experiment was carried out according to the above experimental method. The results are shown in Fig. 7D. It can be seen that the initial concentration of CPBL is too high or too low, it is not conducive to the PEC reaction. This may be due to the high concentration of organic matter in CPBL, resulting in the organic matter to adhere to the surface of the photoanode, which makes the electrode corroded and PEC efficiency decreases. When the concentration of CPBL is low, there is less organic matter, and h+ will be left after the organic matter is oxidized, which will continue to oxidize the photoanode catalyst and eventually reduces the efficiency of PEC.

To study the effect of light source on the PEC degradation of CPBL and H2 production, a 250 W Xe lamp was selected as the light source for PEC test (the experimental conditions are the same as the above test), and the results are shown in Fig. S4. It can be seen that the RTCODCr of CPBL and the yield of H2 under UV light are 1.19 and 1.29 times that under visible light, respectively, indicating that the PEC performance of N-CDs/Cu NRs under UV light is better. This is consistent with the DR UV–vis test results shown in Fig. 5A. Although the composite has a certain absorption intensity in the visible region, the absorption intensity of N-CDs/Cu NRs is still lower than that of UV light.

By analyzing the RTCODCr of CPBL and the amount of H2 in Fig. 7A-D, it is not difficult to find that the variation of RTCODCr and H2 production with time is consistent. When the RTCODCr of CPBL is higher, the H2 production is also higher, indicating that the H2 produced in the cathode chamber may come from the H+ produced by the degradation of CPBL. To further verify this conclusion, we prepared and diluted CPBL with D2O instead of deionized water to obtain D2O-CPBL with the same concentration and pH as the CPBL used in the above experiments. Then, PEC degradation of CPBL and H2 production experiments were performed using N-CDs/Cu NRs. The gas sample generated by the cathode is subjected to isotopic detection, and the results are shown in Fig. 7E. It can be seen that the gases produced by the cathode are H2, HD and D2, in which H2 is the main product. This indicates that the H+ in H2 mainly comes from the degradation of organic matter in CPBL, and a very small amount of H2O decomposition.

As mentioned above, CPBL has a wide range of species and a very complex composition. How do various substances change during its degradation process? In the process of PEC degradation, which substances play a role in consuming h+? To answer these questions, we performed GC–MS tests on the liquid samples from the anode chamber under different reaction times. The qualitative analysis of various chromatographic peaks was performed based on an online automatic search. The results are shown in Fig. 7F and Table S1 (See Table S1 for the names of the substances and the relative percentage content of each chromatographic peak corresponding to Fig. 7F).

It can be seen from Fig. 7F that CPBL (0 min of degradation) has 42 peaks (relative percentage content is greater than 1.0%). After 15 min, 30 min, 45 min and 60 min of PEC degradation, the GC–MS of the water samples showed 29, 22, 18 and 8 peaks, respectively (relative percentage content is greater than 0.2%, the same substances as the original CPBL are still numbered 1–42; the eight new peaks after 15 min are numbered X1-X8; the three new peaks after 30 min are numbered Y1-Y3; the three new peaks after 45 min are numbered Z1-Z3). After 60 min of degradation, the species of organic matter (based on the number of chromatographic peaks) was reduced by 80.95%. We believe that the new substances that appeared after 15 min, 30 min, 45 min and 60 min of PEC degradation were originally present in the original CPBL, but were ignored by us because of the large variety of substances in the original CPBL and the relative percentage content of these substances were less than 1%. Apparently, the CPBL used in this experiment was treated with PEC degradation by N-CDs/Cu NRs, the organic pollutant species were reduced, and the peak intensities were also significantly decreased, indicating that a considerable amount of organic matter in CPBL has been degraded or transformed. According to the analysis of GC–MS data, the total peak area of CPBL (degradation for 0 min) was 64960963.43, and after 60 min of PEC degradation using N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2), the total peak area was 6159936.16. The total removal rate reached 94.82%, which is consistent with the result of the above RTCODCr.

It can be seen from Table S1 that the organic species and contents in CPBL are in order: alkanes > esters > ketones > acids > amines > aldehydes > phenols > ethers. In addition, the GC–MS of the water samples after 0 min, 15 min and 30 min of PEC degradation (Fig. 7F) showed a large bulge in the retention time of 25–50 min (the peak range of ester organics). This indicates that the CPBL used in this experiment has a high ester content. After 45 min of PEC degradation, most of the esters were degraded. After 60 min, the esters were completely degraded.

Further analysis of Fig. 7F shows that after 15 min of reaction, phenols and aldehydes in CPBL were completely degraded. Ketones, alkanes, amines and esters were reduced by 5, 3, 2 and 1 substances, respectively. The ethers remain unchanged, while the acids add a substance, which may be caused by the decomposition of the ester bond. After 30 min of reaction, acids were completely degraded, alkanes and esters were decreased by 4 and 1 substances, respectively, while amines, ketones and ethers remained unchanged. After 45 min of reaction, ethers were completely degraded, alkanes and ketones were reduced by 3 and 1 substances, respectively, while amines and esters remained unchanged. After 60 min of reaction, alkanes and esters were decreased by 5 and 4 substances, respectively, while ketones remained unchanged, and amines were completely degraded. Eventually, there are 4 alkanes, 3 ketones and 1 ester remaining. The above results show that all aldehydes, phenols, acids, amines and ethers in CPBL could be completely degraded when PEC degradation was performed with N-CDs/Cu NRs (SP2) for 60 min. In addition, most alkanes, ketones and esters could be degraded, which means that most of the organics in CPBL could provide H+ to combine with e- to generate H2 during the degradation of PEC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of PEC degradation of CPBL and simultaneous production of H2
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to reveal the mechanism of PEC degradation of CPBL and H2 production, the active species trapping experiments were carried out with SP2. BQ (2 mmol L-1), TA (2 mmol L-1) and EDTA-2Na (2 mmol L-1) were applied to the experiment of PEC degradation of CPBL and H2 production to capture the
O2 –,
OH and h+, respectively, and the results are shown in Fig. S5A. It can be found the degradation efficiency of CPBL was significantly inhibited when TA (
OH scavenger) or BQ (
O2 – scavenger) was added to the reaction. However, the degradation of CPBL was slightly inhibited when EDTA-2Na (h+ scavenger) was added. This indicates that
O2 –,
OH and h+ are the main reactive species in the degradation of PEC, and their effects on the oxidative degradation reaction are
OH >
O2 – > h+.

In addition, we also demonstrated the presence of
O2 – and
OH during the PEC reaction by the NBT method and the benzoic acid hydroxylation method [47], respectively. The
O2 – radical produced in the PEC reaction can react with NBT to form the soluble blue formazan in aqueous solution, and the absorption value of its UV–vis absorption spectrum decreases at 337 nm. Similarly, the generated
OH radicals can react with benzoic acid to yield o-hydroxybenzoic acid, and the o-hydroxybenzoic acid can form complex with the added Fe3+ ion, and the absorption value of its UV–vis absorption spectrum increases at 480 nm. It can be seen from Fig. S5B that after adding NBT in the process of PEC degradation of CPBL, the light absorption intensity of the reaction solution is significantly reduced after 60 min of reaction. If benzoic acid and Fe3+ are added, the light absorbance intensity of the reaction solution will increase significantly (Fig. S5C). This result is consistent with the results shown in Fig. S5A. These results further confirm that the
O2 – and
OH radicals are generated during the PEC reactions.

Combining the aforementioned photoelectric properties of N-CDs/Cu NRs and the experimental results of degradation of CPBL and H2 production by PEC, the mechanism of degradation of CPBL and H2 production by PEC using N-CDs/Cu NRs was speculated, as shown in Fig. 8 .

(i) When the catalyst surface is irradiated by light, the N-CDs/Cu NRs absorb photons and induce the LSPR effect on the surface of the Cu NRs region. This process can generate hot electrons and transfer them to the coal-based N-CDs region (conduction band); at the same time, the coal-based N-CDs region of the catalyst also absorbs some photons and is excited to produce photogenerated e- and h+ (Eq. (3)). (ii) The generated h+ degrades CPBL according to Eq. (4), producing CO2, H2O, H+ and other small organic compounds; meanwhile, h+ can also oxidize H2O in the system according to Eqs. (5) and (6) to produce
O2 –,
OH and h+;
OH is consumed by CPBL as an oxidant together with h+ (Eq. (7)). (iii) Under the applied bias voltage, e- are transferred to the cathode and combine with H+ (mainly from organic degradation) passing through the proton exchange membrane to form H2, as in Eq. (8); a small amount of e- remaining at the anode react with the O2 generated by the decomposition of H2O to form
O2 – (Eq. (9)), which together with
OH and h+, degrade CPBL according to Eq. (10).
N-CDs/Cu NRs + hν → h+ + e-(3)
CPBL + h+ → CO2 + H2O + H+ + Other products(4)

h+ + H2O →
OH + H+(5)

CPBL +
OH → CO2 + H2O + H+ + Other products(7)

CPBL +
O2 – → CO2 + H2O + H+ + Other products(10)

Obviously, the degradation of CPBL and H2 production by PEC using N-CDs/Cu NRs is a dual process: at the anode, free radicals such as
O2 –,
OH and h+ oxidize CPBL to produce H+, CO2, H2O and other small molecules; at the cathode, H+ is reduced by e- to produce H2. In this process, CPBL is degraded, which not only solves the environmental pollution caused by CPBL, but also provides H+ for the preparation of H2. Therefore, theenvironmental protectionconcept of “turning waste into treasure “can be truly realized through the method shown in this paper.

In summary, a novel Cu NRs-supported coal-based N-CDs composite nanocatalyst was prepared by a facile method. To investigate the performance of N-CDs/Cu NRs for the degradation of CPBL and simultaneous production of H2, we designed an H-type photoelectrocatalytic (PEC) reactor. The results showed that N-CDs/Cu NRs as an easy to prepared, efficient and stable catalytic material, exhibited excellent catalytic performance in the degradation of CPBL and the production of H2. In addition, the oxidation reaction to degrade the wastewater with high CODCr and the reduction reaction to produce H2 are carried out simultaneously. GC–MS results showed that all aldehydes, phenols, acids, amines, ethers, and most alkanes, ketones and esters in CPBL could be degraded after 60 min. In this process, the organic matter in CPBL acts as a sacrificial agent to ensure the effective progress of the H2 reaction. This research could provide some ideas for promising applications, including the development of photoelectrocatalysts, wastewater treatment and H2 production.

Jinyan Sun: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Halidan Maimaiti: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Bo Xu: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Lirong Feng: Writing – review & editing. Jianzhao Bao: Writing – review & editing. Xuwei Zhao: Writing – review & editing.
